nativeRange.habitat: Wet, shaded margins, seeps, and stream banks in the southeastern and eastern United States; reported to tolerate seasonal emersion and is regularly grown emersed in greenhouse culture

decorPreferences: open midground or trimmed foreground space
moderate to strong lighting for compact, bushy form
stable water column with regular liquid fertilisation

avoidWith: boisterous diggers that uproot stem plants

summary: Baby Tears Giant is a bright-green, small-leaved stem plant from wet, shaded margins of the eastern United States. Known scientifically as Micranthemum umbrosum, it produces noticeably larger, rounder leaves than related species like Pearl Weed. It generally performs best with moderate to strong light, a nutrient-rich substrate, and supplemental CO2.
careNotes: Baby Tears Giant (Micranthemum umbrosum) — also sold as 'Giant Baby Tears' or 'Shade Mudflower' — is a creeping, branching stem plant in the family Linderniaceae native to wet, shaded margins of the southeastern and eastern United States. It is one of three Micranthemum species commonly found in aquascaping and is the largest-leaved of the group: its leaves are noticeably larger and rounder than those of M. micranthemoides (Pearl Weed) and considerably larger than the tiny-leaved Hemianthus callitrichoides (Dwarf Baby Tears). Typical submerged growth reaches 5–15 cm when kept trimmed; untrimmed specimens can extend to 20 cm or beyond. Lighting is best at medium-to-strong levels: under low light, stems tend to stretch with longer gaps between leaves, while stronger light encourages compact, bushy growth and lets the plant serve as a trimmed foreground carpet. A nutrient-rich substrate and regular water-column fertilisation with iron are generally advised. CO2 supplementation is recommended — growth tends to slow noticeably without it, though the plant can survive at reduced CO2 levels. Baby Tears Giant is versatile in layout: consistent pruning keeps stems low and encourages horizontal runners suitable for foreground and midground placement, while leaving stems untrimmed allows upward growth that fills midground and background zones. Initial adaptation after planting can be slow, but specimens generally become more undemanding once established.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and generally straightforward. Trim healthy stem tops just above a node, group several cuttings together, and replant them in the substrate approximately 1–2 cm apart; they tend to root and begin branching within days. Mature plants can also be divided, or daughter plants removed from established mats and replanted to thicken the bed.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Buce Plant product page for Micranthemum umbrosum (Giant Baby Tears). Provided: family Scrophulariaceae (older sense; now Linderniaceae), height 2–6 in (~5–15 cm), medium light, CO2 optional (~6–14 ppm beneficial), medium growth, North America origin, easy care, propagation via stem cuttings, versatile foreground/midground/background placement, and the comparison to Pearl Weed with slightly larger and rounder leaves.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked: high light (especially for low lawn-like growth), CO2 not required but beneficial, medium-to-fast growth, temperature 72–86 °F (≈22–30 °C), foreground placement (also floating), easy-to-moderate care, propagation via cuttings replanted in plugs.

additional.name: Aquasabi Plantbase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: family Linderniaceae, common name 'Shade mudflower / Giant baby tears', medium difficulty, foreground/midground placement (also nano), fast growth, 10–30 cm care height (used as upper bound), medium-to-strong lighting (≥0.5 W/L), pH 5–7, gH 0–30 °dGH, kH 0–10 °dKH, CO2 recommended (growth significantly decreases without). Slow initial adaptation, then undemanding.

additional.name: Tropica
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 'medium' difficulty, elegant small-leaved stem plant, moderate growth, vivid bright green colour, suitable for small aquariums. Tropica did not surface a public detail page with parameter numbers, so we lean on Aquasabi and Modern Aquarium for the numeric values.

additional.name: Wikipedia (genus Micranthemum)
additional.notes: Used only for taxonomy fallback: confirmed genus Micranthemum in family Linderniaceae, order Lamiales, with native range 'Eastern USA to Tropical and Subtropical America'. Species page for M. umbrosum specifically was not available.
